nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still clear water with dense surface and overhanging vegetation; shaded forest streams, pools, and margins of the Congo River basin

decorPreferences: dense planting with broad-leafed plants (Amazon sword, Anubias)
floating plants (frogbit, water sprite) to support bubble-nest building
driftwood and leaf litter for cover and tannin release
dim lighting or surface shade to reduce stress
tightly fitting lid — strong escape risk

diet.notes: Primarily accepts small live and frozen foods — bloodworm, daphnia, brine shrimp, mosquito larvae, and small insects. Dry foods are rarely taken and many individuals refuse them entirely; a varied live/frozen diet is essential for long-term health and conditioning for breeding. Wild fish feed on small insects, crustaceans, and invertebrates at or near the surface.

goodWith: Congo tetras (similar water parameters, mid-to-upper zone)
medium-sized barbs (robust enough to avoid harassment)
small peaceful rasboras and tetras of comparable size
dwarf cichlids in adequately large tanks
avoidWith: long-finned or slow-moving species (fin damage risk from conspecific-style aggression)
multiple males in small tanks (intense territorial fights)
very boisterous or nippy species that outcompete at feeding

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase reference image for Microctenopoma fasciolatum; verify photographer attribution on the FishBase species page before use.

summary: The Banded Bushfish is a small (to 8 cm) Congo Basin anabantid placed in Microctenopoma after Norris’s 1995 genus revision. Males develop spectacular filamentous extensions to the dorsal and anal fins. A bubble-nesting labyrinth fish, it requires live or frozen micro-foods and a tightly lidded, well-planted aquarium.
careNotes:
Microctenopoma fasciolatum is a small labyrinth fish native to the lower and central Congo River basin, including DR Congo, Republic of Congo, and a handful of rivers in Cameroon. It was reassigned from Ctenopoma to the genus Microctenopoma by Norris in 1995, a revision reflecting consistent morphological and reproductive differences from the egg-scattering Ctenopoma species.

In the aquarium, a minimum of 80 L with a 75 cm footprint is recommended for a pair; additional space (100+ L) is needed to house more than two adults given the territorial nature of males. The tank must have a tight-fitting lid — this species is a skilled jumper and will exploit any gap. Dense planting with floating cover (frogbit, water sprite) is essential both for security and to provide anchor points for the male’s bubble nest. Subdued lighting, supplemented with driftwood and leaf litter, replicates the shaded, tannin-stained water of its native streams and reduces stress-related colour fading.

Water chemistry should be soft and slightly acidic to neutral: pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 4–20 dH, temperature 24–28°C. The species is sensitive to deteriorating water quality and should be kept in a well-filtered but low-flow environment. Regular partial water changes are important.

Diet is the most common husbandry challenge: most specimens refuse dry food, especially on arrival. Offer bloodworm, daphnia, brine shrimp, and small mosquito larvae as the dietary staple. Some individuals can be gradually weaned onto high-quality frozen preparations, but live food should remain available for conditioning and long-term vitality. Trophic level data (FishBase: 3.3) confirms a primarily carnivorous diet.

Males are distinguishable by their dramatically elongated, filamentous dorsal and anal fins — greatly extended compared to females’ rounded fins — and in some populations display iridescent blue spots on these fin extensions during courtship. The body pattern of bold dark vertical bars on a pale ground intensifies with age and in breeding condition. Females have shorter, plain-margined fins. Conspecific aggression between males can cause significant fin damage in confined quarters; maintain only one male per tank unless the aquarium is large and heavily planted with visual breaks.
breedingNotes:
Microctenopoma fasciolatum is a bubble-nesting anabantid, unlike the egg-scattering Leopard Bush Fish (Ctenopoma acutirostre). Breeding is rated difficult in captivity. Condition the pair with live foods for several weeks before attempting spawning. Dedicated breeding setup: 40–60 L, soft water (2–4 dGH), pH ~6.5, temperature raised to 28–29°C, and abundant floating plants or broad-leafed stem plants near the surface. Reduce surface agitation completely.

The male builds a moderately complex bubble nest among floating vegetation or anchored under a broad leaf. After typical anabantoid embrace (male wraps around female beneath the nest), 500–1,000 eggs are deposited and collected into the nest by the male. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours; fry become free-swimming after a further 2–3 days. The male aggressively guards the nest — remove the female after spawning; remove the male once fry are free-swimming to prevent predation.

Fry are very small and require infusoria or commercial first foods (paramecia, rotifers) for the first 5–7 days before graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii and microworms. Brood sizes of hundreds of fry are possible, often requiring culling unless a large dedicated rearing tank is available. Fry can be slowly weaned to prepared foods over several weeks.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Microctenopoma fasciolatum
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (8 cm SL), temperature (24–28°C), pH (6.5–7.5), hardness (4–20 dH), minimum tank dimensions (30×12×12” / 75×30×30 cm), native range (Congo basin incl. Cameroon), habitat description, diet notes (live/frozen preferred, dry rarely taken), bubble-nest breeding account, fry care, and escape-risk warning.

additional.name: FishBase — Microctenopoma fasciolatum
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Anabantidae, Anabantiformes), max length (8.0 cm TL), temperature (24–28°C), pH (6.5–7.5), hardness (4–20 dH), native range (lower and middle Congo River basin, Cameroon, DR Congo, Republic of Congo), trophic level 3.3 (carnivore), IUCN Least Concern, facultative air-breathing noted.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Microctenopoma fasciolatum
additional.notes: Confirmed water parameters, bubble-nest breeding details (egg hatch 24 h, free-swimming 2–3 days post-hatch, fry require infusoria then BBS nauplii), escape-risk note, and tankmate suggestions (hatchetfish, corydoras). Breeding rated difficult.

additional.name: Diszhal.info — Microctenopoma fasciolatum
additional.notes: Confirmed lifespan 5–8 years, minimum tank 80 L (pair 100 L), breeding water parameters (2–4 dGH, pH 6.5, 28°C), clutch 500–700 eggs, male fin extensions described as similar to Paradise Fish.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser GmbH — Microctenopoma fasciolatum
additional.notes: Confirmed Congo basin origin (including Lac Mai Ndombe), adult size ~8–9 cm, male spotted fins vs plain female fins, note that specimens rarely appear in trade, and that aggression between individuals resembles Paradise Fish. Maintenance temperature given as 22–26°C (slightly cooler than other sources; breeding temperature 24–28°C adopted per SeriouslyFish and FishBase consensus).

fish.breedingNotes: Bubble-nester; male builds nest among floating plants, embraces female below nest, guards 500–1,000 eggs. Eggs hatch ~24–36 h; fry free-swimming after 2–3 more days. Requires soft, acidic water and live-food conditioning. Fry need infusoria before graduating to BBS nauplii.
